description:
# Stop creating narrow specs that compile identically to wider ones

## The smell, in plain English

For our 6-line ast_eval program, the typer specialized `eval` into
9 separate fns:

    eval(any)           eval(int)
    eval(2)             eval({:num, 2})
    eval(3)             eval({:num, 3})
    eval(4)             eval({:num, 4})
    eval({:add, ...})

The `eval(2)`, `eval(3)`, `eval(4)` specs buy nothing — their
narrowed input doesn't change the emitted code vs. `eval(any)`,
because the value 2 doesn't fold any TypeTest, doesn't change any
ABI repr, and doesn't enable any fast path. They're pure code
bloat, multiplied 4× through their continuations (~20 dead specs).

## What's already there (don't reinvent)

`SpecRegistry::resolve` (src/spec_registry.rs) already supports
**subsumption dispatch**: a narrow callsite query that doesn't have
its own spec falls through to a covering wider spec. So we don't
need to register narrow specs to make dispatch work — the wider
spec is the safe fallback.

## What changes

Add a **payoff predicate** before registering a new (callee,
narrow_key) spec in the typer:

    fn narrower_spec_pays_off(narrow_key, wider_key, &type_info) -> bool

The narrower spec is created iff at least one of:

  - **ABI change**: the narrow Descr maps a param or return to a
    different ArgRepr (Tagged → RawInt/RawF64 or vice versa).
  - **TypeTest fold**: some TypeTest inside the body becomes
    statically true or false under the narrow Descr where it
    couldn't under the wider one. (Computable by re-walking the
    body's TypeTest sites against the narrow vs. wider input
    Descrs.)
  - **Typed fast-path eligibility**: a BinOp or cmp gains a typed
    fast path (the VR.1-5 paths). Same shape of check.

If none, **route to the wider spec** instead of creating a new one.
Existing subsumption dispatch handles it.

## Atomic steps

1. Implement the predicate as a pure function over (narrow_key,
   wider_key, &FnIr, &type info), unit-tested in isolation.
2. Find the registration site(s) — primarily
   `drain_and_register` in src/ir_typer.rs (line ~664) — and gate
   each insertion through the predicate. Pass the existing any-key
   spec's key as wider_key.
3. Regression-bless: re-bless ast_eval golden; verify other
   fixture goldens unchanged.
4. Document the predicate next to the registration site so future
   readers know why a narrowing might not produce a spec.

## What's removed

- ast_eval's value-narrow int specs (`eval(2)`, `eval(3)`,
  `eval(4)`, `eval(int)` if redundant with eval(any)) and their
  continuation cascades.
- Any analogous specs in other programs that the predicate
  identifies as payoff-free. (Today: probably none in existing
  fixtures.)
- ~hundreds of lines from ast_eval/expected.clif.

## Acceptance

- ast_eval golden re-blessed; spec count drops from 36 toward
  ~10. `fz dump` of ast_eval shows only the specs that actually
  change codegen.
- All 154 existing fixture matrix trials still pass. Their
  goldens are either byte-identical (most fixtures don't narrow
  aggressively) or strictly shorter (none expected to grow).
- New unit test for the predicate covering each of the three
  payoff axes.

## Critical risk

Predicate too narrow → miss an optimization (some downstream pass
relies on narrow Descrs we now route around). Mitigation: on
doubt, KEEP the narrow spec. The predicate is a *gate* added to
permissive behavior, not a wholesale rewrite. If a regression
appears, widen the predicate to cover the missed case.

## Out of scope

- The recursive-return wrong-type bug (fz-ul4.41) — orthogonal.
- Reachability prune (fz-ul4.42) — complements but separate.
- Decision-tree dispatch (fz-ul4.43) — separate.
title: typer: payoff-gated spec creation (drop narrowings that change nothing)
